So what in the world is net neutrality and why are people so passionate about it?
Here's the explanation:
The "net" refers to the Internet.
The "neutrality" part is about keeping the Internet as it is today.
It is a set of rules that went into effect in 2015 to prevent speed traps on the Internet.
In other words speeding up access to some sites, slowing down access to others or
blocking certain sites entirely.
The companies that deliver your Internet like Comcast, Verizon and AT&T have spent millions
to get rid of net neutrality.
They argue that having the government micromanage the business
is not good for them or their consumers.
On the other side are Internet giants like Facebook and Google,

However there is a "worst-case" scenario location area from Northern California to Pennsylvania.
The space agency predicted the space station will reenter Earth's atmosphere between March
30 and April 3.
Authorities say Parkland, Florida shooter Nikolas Cruz is actually receiving fan mail.
Hundreds of letters are coming in from all over the US and Europe from young girls, women
and even grown men.
Some are typed, others handwritten on notebook paper.
There are drawings, cards and envelopes filled with pictures of women and even teens in suggestive
poses.
Cruz hasn't seen any of the mail because he doesn't have access to it.
Unless his lawyers or family tell him, he has no way of knowing these letters exists.
The number of companies are cutting ties with the National Rifle Association (NRA) continues
to grow.
The latest companies are Delta and United Airlines.
Both companies announced they are no longer offering discounted rates to NRA members and
Delta has requested that the NRA remove its information from their website.
"True Car" announced on Twitter that it will end its car buying service relationship with
the NRA at the end of this month.
After the school shooting in Florida last week, many people demanded stronger gun laws.
NRA supporters disagreed. As a result, many companies ended their partnerships with the organization.
Several rental companies including Enterprise, Hertz and Avis, say they are ending discount
programs with the NRA.
Insurance giant MetLife will no longer offer home and auto policy discounts for
NRA members.
And the First National Bank of Omaha is pledging to no longer issue NRA-branded Visa cards.
The bank says it made its decision due to what it called "customer feedback" but did
not elaborate.
The House and Senate were back in session today attempting to break an impasse that
caused federal agencies to shut down.
And there is no indication lawmakers are any closer to reaching a deal.
Chamber rules require 60 votes to break the budget impasse and Senate Republicans currently
have 51 votes.
President Trump was up and tweeting early Sunday morning.
He tweeted "Great to see how hard Republicans are fighting for our military and safety at
the border.
The Dems just want illegal immigrants to pour into our nation unchecked.
If stalemate continues, Republicans should go to 51 percent -- "nuclear option" -- and
vote on real, long-term budget, no C.R.'s."
